segments.text: Microscopic silicon-based lateral high-aspect-ratio structures for thin film conformality analysis
We show that the leading front of the thickness pro /uniFB01 le in high-aspect-ratio structures gives direct information on the sticking probabilities of the reactants under most conditions.

Atomic layer deposition (ALD), which makes use of selflimiting precursor and coreactant steps, 1 -3 has become an enabling technique for the preparation of ultrathin /uniFB01 lms on the increasingly demanding 3D structures used in applications such as memory cells and transistors.
4,5 The self-limiting nature of ALD ideally yields a constant thickness of a /uniFB01 lm deposited over a high-aspect-ratio (AR) substrate.
This work provides a method to directly extract initial sticking probabilities from thickness pro /uniFB01 les acquired in high AR structures.
Here, we note that the slope of the leading front of the thickness pro /uniFB01 le in such structures is determined by the reactivity of the reactant that penetrates the structure the least deep.
## A. High-aspect-ratio structures for conformality analysis
11 several high AR structures have been used in the literature to assess the conformality of ALD processes, such as vertical trenches, 16 -19 pillars, 20 -23 and porous materials.
24,25 Alternatively, lateral structures can be employed 6,7,26,27 which allow for top-view diagnostics to easily and accurately quantify the conformality and properties of the deposited /uniFB01 lm.
The microscopic lateral-high-aspect-ratio (LHAR) trenches developed by Puurunen and co-workers, 7,14,28,29 named as PillarHall ® technology, have been adopted in this work.
In the used third generation LHAR structures (LHAR3), a polysilicon membrane, supported by a network of Si pillars, is suspended above a c-Si substrate with a nominal gap height of 500 nm.
Elongated openings in the membrane allow for diffusion of the ALD reactants into the lateral trench up to a depth of 5 mm, corresponding to an AR of 10 000.
In such an extremely high AR feature, the reactants typically do not reach the end of the feature during deposition, resulting in a diffusion-limited thickness pro /uniFB01 le.
30 Figure 1(c) gives a schematic cross-
sectional side view of an LHAR3 structure, also showing a typical pro /uniFB01 le of a /uniFB01 lm grown by ALD.

During this longer saturation time, the reactant diffuses deeper into the feature, resulting in a broader diffusion front.
For relatively high values of s 0 , e.g., 10 /C0 2 , the surface saturates quickly when the reactant diffuses inward, resulting in a sharp pro /uniFB01 le front.

While Eq. (3) generally applies to trench structures, it can be further generalized by using the equivalent aspect ratio (EAR) 11 of the structure of interest instead of ~ z .
For example, EAR ¼ ð z = 2 h Þ ¼ ð ~ z = 2 Þ for a trench, while EAR ¼ z = r for a pore with radius r .

In this work, the lower and upper limits of s 0 are computed assuming <10% variation in reactant dosing and ∼ 10 nm uncertainty in cavity height.
Broadening of the front due to the narrowing of the trench during deposition (400 cycles, ∼ 46 nm Al2O3) has a virtually negligible effect on the pro /uniFB01 les reported in this work.

measures s 0 by monitoring the surface chemistry, while our LHAR method is based on the resulting /uniFB01 lm thickness.

This impact of soft-saturation on /uniFB01 lm conformality is typically not experimentally observed when using a structure with a relatively low AR, where the reactant dose is approximately constant throughout the feature.
In conclusion, we have established a method to directly determine sticking probabilities during ALD from thickness pro /uniFB01 les obtained in high-aspect-ratio structures.
While in this work, lateral-high-aspect-ratio trenches were used, the method can be applied to other 3D features as well.
